content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 1, 1977


77-06-248

James P. Jackson
City Attorney
City of Sacramento
812 Tenth Street, Suite 201
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-015

Dear Mr. Jackson:

On behalf of Councilman Michael S. Sands, you seek an opinion with respect to the Councilman's responsibilities under Government Code Section 87100, the disqualification requirements of the Political Reform Act. Since your letter raises no substantial questions involving an interpretation of the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful.

The facts as I understand them are as follows: The Councilman is an attorney whose law practice is devoted almost exclusively to the practice of criminal law. The issue before the City Council, which forms the basis for your opinion request, is whether the City Council should pass a resolution prohibiting city staff from destroying internal investigation reports prepared by the City Police Department. Internal investigation reports are prepared on the basis of citizen complaints about actions taken by a city police officer. Pursuant to California law, such reports may be discovered by a defendant in a criminal case if the defendant has been arrested for a crime in a police officer and the defendant is claiming self defense.

As presented, the conflict of interest issue requires an examination of Government Code Section 87103 which provides, inter alia:

Financial Interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonable foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

James F. Jackson
Page two
June 1, 1977


     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);
...

     Under Section 87103, several elements must be pre-
sented before a public official is required to disqualify him-
self from participating in a governmental decision.  First, the
public official must possess a financial interest within the
meaning of Section 87103(a), above.  Second, it must be rea-
sonable foreseeable that the governmental decision will have
an effect on the financial interest.  Third, the governmental
decision must materially affect the financial interest.  Fourth,
the governmental decision's effect on the financial interest
of the public official must be distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally.1/  Pursuant to the facts provided in
your letter, it is doubtful whether the decision in question
will produce the effects set forth in elements three and four
above.  However, we need not reach these issues because the
first element concerning "foreseeability" does not appear to
be satisfied.  In Councilman Sands' situation, it is not fore-
seeable that the approval or disapproval of the proposed gen-
eral resolution regarding the destruction of citizen complaint
records will financially affect the Councilman's law practice.
From the facts presented, there is no basis for concluding that
the passage or defeat of the resolution will affect the number
of clients, the amount or size of fees, or the capability of
the firm to generate either more fees or clients.  In analyz-
ing the concept "foreseeably," the Commission issued a policy
statement which provides that:

     ...the term requires the application of reason-
     able judgment to assess the degree of likelihood
     that the decision made or participated in will
     affect a financial interest.  Where the likeli-
     hood is sufficiently great that a reasonable
     person would predict or anticipate an effect on
     a financial interest, the effect of the decision
     is foreseeable.2/

     It is the absence of the predictive or anticipatory
effects which forecloses a conclusion that a decision on the
resolution would foreseeably affect Councilman Sands' law

_______________

     1/  See Opinion requested by Tom Thorner, on behalf
of the Board of Directors, Marin Municipal Water District,
1 FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975).

     2/  Policy Statement, Fair Political Practices Com-
mission, issued August, 1976.

James P. Jackson
Page three
June 1, 1977


practice. Thus, it is concluded that the Councilman may
participate in decisions related to the proposed resolution
before the Sacramento City Council Law and Legislative Com-
mittee regarding the destruction of internal investigation
section reports.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321. If I may be of further assistance to you in this mat-
ter, please feel free to contact me at (916)322-6444.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:jo

77 015
Request No.______________

CITY OF SACRAMENTO Data Received ___5/11/77___

Response Due_______________________
JAMES E. HARRISON
CITY ATTORNEY

THEODORE H. KOBEY, JR.
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

DEPARTMENT OF LAW LELIAND J. SAVAGE
STEVEN R. MEYERS
SACRAMENTO, CALIF. 95814 TERENCE M. BROWN
SUITE 201 TELEPHONE (916) 449-5346 ELIZABETH HASSARD SILVER
S. RUSSELL SELIX, JR.
DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEYS

May 6, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Legal Opinion Request

Dear Commission Members:

I am writing to ask the Commission for a legal opinion
concerning the application of the Political Reform Act of 1974,
as amended, to a factual situation existing in the City of
Sacramento.

The facts are as follows:

The Sacramento City Council recently referred a proposed
resolution to the City Council Law and Legislation Committee for
a report. The proposed resolution would amend the City resolution
on destruction of public records by adding a provision prohibiting
the city staff from destroying Internal Investigation Section
(hereinafter IIS) reports prepared by the City Police Department.
A copy of the proposed resolution is attached for your information.

The IIS reports are prepared when a citizen complains about
actions taken by a city police officer. The material in the
reports is usually obtained with the assurance that it will be
kept in confidence. In accordance with the case of Pitchess v.
Superior Court, (11 Cal. 3rd 531) the IIS reports may be discovered
by the defendant in a criminal case if the defendant has been
arrested for assault on a police officer and the defendant is
claiming self-defense. The records are discoverable if they show
a prior history of excessive force by the police officer. After
the defendant makes a motion to discover the records, an in-camera
hearing is conducted by the court to review the IIS records and
determine which records are material or will lead to the relevant
evidence. If the court decides that certain records are relevant,
then the City may assert its privilege under Evidence Code Section
1040 to keep the records from being discovered. However, if this
privilege is asserted by the City, the court may give an instruc-
tion to the jury in the case that the particular police officer
involved has used excessive force on one or more occasions in
the past.

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1977
Page 2


Acting pursuant to Government Code Section 34090 and
Resolution No. 76-181, the Police Department, in June, 1976,
destroyed IIS files prepared prior to June, 1974. (excepting case
files relating to pending civil or criminal litigation cases
involving terminations or resignations). Under Resolution 76-181
city records more than two years old can be destroyed, with the
approval of the City Manager and City Attorney. Such consent was
given for the destruction of the IIS files prepared prior to June,
1974. In the later part of 1976, however, the City Attorney
withdrew his consent to the destruction of IIS records until the
matter could be further considered by the City Council.

The resolution referred by the Council to the Law and
Legislation Committee would impose a permanent prohibition on the
destruction of IIS records. However, it is possible that the
committee or the Council would propose that the records be
retained only for five or ten years rather than forever.

Councilman Michael S. Sands is the Chairman of the Council
Law and Legislation Committee. He is also an attorney who
specializes in criminal law. Approximately 95 per cent of
Councilman Sands' law practice is in the criminal law field. He
is a certified specialist in the practice of criminal law. At
the present time Councilman Sands does not accept as clients any
defendant who is accused of assault against a City or Sacramento
peace officer. Cases of this type involving Sheriff's deputies
or Highway Patrol officers are accepted by Mr. Sands, however.
In addition, Mr. Sands' term on the City Council will expire on
about December 1, 1977, and he has announced that he will not be
a candidate for re-election. It is reasonably foreseeable that
Mr. Sands will accept assault cases involving City police officers
after his present term expires.

The question being asked the Commission is whether Councilman
Sands can vote on the proposed resolution. At the present time the
resolution is being held in abeyance and Mr. Sands has stated that
he will not vote on the issue unless he gets an opinion from the
Fair Political Practices Commission permitting him to do so.

The issue, it appears to me, is whether Mr. Sands is prohibited
from acting on the resolution by the provisions of Government Code
Section 87100. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision on
the resolution will have a material financial effect on Mr. Sands'
law practice? Mr. Sands thinks that it is not reasonably fore-
seeable because the effect of either approving or disapproving
the resolution cannot be known at this time. If the resolution is
approved and no further destruction of records takes place, then
attorneys, including Mr. Sands, who represent criminal defendants
in this type of case would have access to more IIS reports. Over

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1977
Page 3

a period of time they will be able to see more and more IIS files,
unless the City asserts its privilege under Evidence Code Section
1040. Because of the attorney's ability to examine these files
he may be better able to defend his client. On the other hand, if
the IIS files continue to be destroyed, then the criminal defense
attorney will have fewer IIS files relating to the police officer
and thus fewer opportunities to defend on the basis of prior
excessive force by the officer. However, if these records continue
to be destroyed, it is possible that a court might find a
dismissal must be entered for the defendant on the basis that the
public agency has suppressed relevant evidence in the criminal case
by destroying the IIS files. Litigation currently pending against
the City on this issue. The pending litigation involves the
question of whether the case of People v. Hitch, 12 Cal. 3d 641, is
applicable. If so, sanctions may be imposed upon the prosecution
and the pending criminal case may be dismissed. Thus, it is really
impossible to know at this time the accurate effect of action on
the pending resolution.

     Mr. Sands also believes that there is no conflict because it is
unlikely that his action, or the Council action, on the resolution
will have the result of bringing additional business to him.
Regardless of whether Mr. Sands is successful in defending these
cases against City police officers, other defense attorneys will
be able to use the same law and may be either more or less
successful than Mr. Sands. Thus, while it is reasonably foreseeable
that Mr. Sands will obtain clients in these cases as a result of
his leaving the City Council, it is not foreseeable that he would
obtain any clients because of his action on the resolution.

     In addition, it is my understanding that criminal defense
attorneys charge fees based on an estimate of the time they will
spend on the particular case. No contingent fee is charged based
on whether the attorney wins or loses the case. Councilman Sands
charges fees in accordance with this practice. In fact, the
American Bar Association Code of Professional Responsibility,
Section EC2-20 condemns contingent fee arrangements in criminal
cases. Thus, Councilman Sands would not receive a greater fee
from a defendant in a given case if he wins that case because of
the availability of more IIS files.

     To sum up, the question asked is whether Councilman Sands,
who will probably represent defendants in future cases involving
assault against a City police officer, may vote on a pending
resolution which may have the effect of increasing his ability to
defend future clients.

     Although it is not part of this question, you should be aware
that there are two other attorneys on the City Council who are
engaged in the general practice of law. From time to time they

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 6, 1977
Page 4

handle criminal cases. If you determine that Councilman Sands
has a conflict of interest in the above factual situation,
then we will need to inquire of you further as to what degree
the other attorneys on the Council must disqualify themselves
on Council matters relating to criminal law.

We would appreciate your reply to this inquiry at your
earliest convenience.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
JAMES P. JACKSON
City Attorney

JPJ:plf

RESOLUTION No.

Adopted by The Sacramento City Council on date of

March 8, 1977


RESOLUTION AMENDING RESOLUTION NO. 76-131
ADOPTED APRIL 13, 1976 EXCEPTING CERTAIN
RECORDS FROM DESTRUCTION


BE IT RESOLVED BY THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SACRAMENTO:

Resolution No. 76-181, adopted by the Sacramento City

Council on April 13, 1976, is hereby amended by adding sub-

paragraph (f) to paragraph 2 to read as follows:

"(f) records of investigations conducted by
the Internal Investigations Section of
the Sacramento Police Department."


_________________________________
MAYOR

ATTEST:


_________________________________
CITY CLERK

sections.conclusion: It is concluded that the councilman may participate in decisions related to the proposed resolution before the Sacramento City Council Law and Legislative Committee regarding the destruction of internal investigation section reports. In Councilman Sands' situation, it is not foreseeable that the approval or disapproval of the proposed general resolution regarding the destruction of citizen complaint records will financially affect the Councilman's law practice. From the facts presented, there is no basis for concluding that the passage or defeat of the resolution will affect the number of clients, the amount or size of fees, or the capability of the firm to generate either more fees or clients.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Councilman Sands, a criminal defense attorney, vote on a resolution that would prohibit destruction of police internal investigation reports, given that such reports could be discoverable in future criminal cases he might handle?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Councilman Sands may vote on the resolution because it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on his law practice. The first element of the disqualification test—foreseeability—is not satisfied, as there is no basis to predict the resolution will affect client numbers, fees, or his firm's revenue-generating capacity.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality issues present but document is readable. This is an informal opinion letter (not formal opinion) responding to Opinion Request 77-016. The incoming request letter is included at end of document showing full factual background. No formal CONCLUSION section heading, but conclusion clearly stated in body of response letter.

CONCLUSION: It is concluded that the councilman may participate in decisions related to the proposed resolution before the Sacramento City Council Law and Legislative Committee regarding the destruction of internal investigation section reports. In Councilman Sands' situation, it is not foreseeable that the approval or disapproval of the proposed general resolution regarding the destruction of citizen complaint records will financially affect the Councilman's law practice. From the facts presented, there is no basis for concluding that the passage or defeat of the resolution will affect the number of clients, the amount or size of fees, or the capability of the firm to generate either more fees or clients.

embedding.summary: The FPPC issued an informal opinion regarding whether Councilman Michael S. Sands must disqualify himself from voting on a proposed resolution prohibiting destruction of police internal investigation reports, given his criminal law practice. The FPPC concluded Sands may participate because the decision's financial effect on his practice is not reasonably foreseeable.
